doc.title: Lessee — Guaranteed Residual Value
doc.event: Nolan Co. leases equipment for 3 years with payments of $40,000 due at the beginning of each year at a 7% rate. Nolan guarantees a $15,000 residual value; the expected residual value at lease-end is $10,000, so Nolan expects to owe $5,000 under the guarantee. The PV of the annual payments is $112,321 and the PV of $5,000 due in 3 years is $4,082. Prepare Nolan's commencement entry.

lines.why: PV of the three payments (112,321) plus PV of the $5,000 Nolan expects to actually owe (4,082).
lines.trap: Only the expected SHORTFALL enters the liability — not the full $15,000 guarantee.

lines.why: Same total on the obligation side.

memorize.body: Lessee includes a residual guarantee only to the extent it EXPECTS to owe: guarantee − expected residual value.
An UNguaranteed residual never enters the lessee's numbers at all — it matters only to the lessor.

questions.prompt: Guarantee $15,000, expected residual value $10,000. What undiscounted amount from the guarantee enters Nolan's lease liability?

distractors.feedback: You capitalized the full guarantee. Include only the amount PROBABLE of being owed: 15,000 − 10,000 = 5,000.
That's the expected residual VALUE of the asset — the liability piece is the expected shortfall (5,000).
A guaranteed residual can't be ignored when a shortfall is expected — the probable $5,000 payment is part of the lease liability.

build.scaffold: One entry, two lines — the work is upstream: PV of payments + PV of the expected shortfall only.
